source_title: Claim Extraction Pilot Report — 2026-03-04

claims.text: The Covalence Claims Architecture v2 divides the knowledge system into two independently maintained domains: the Epistemological Domain (sources and node sections) and the Semantic Domain (claims and articles).
In Covalence Claims Architecture v2, claims are stored in the existing `nodes` table with `node_type = 'claim'`, requiring no new database tables.
Covalence claims use a SHA-256 content_hash of the canonical claim text as the primary deduplication key.
Covalence's claim deduplication applies a cosine similarity threshold of 0.95 for exact duplicates, and a 0.85–0.94 band for near-duplicates requiring LLM review.
Entity-gated deduplication in Covalence only compares claims for deduplication when they share the same primary entity.
The Covalence Claims Layer Spec uses `node_type = 'claim'` in the existing `nodes` table rather than creating new tables, preserving existing embedding, FTS, and graph traversal infrastructure.
The Covalence Claims Layer Spec depends on covalence#137 (confidence propagation at article level), which ships first; claims refine confidence to the claim level later.
The blue-green migration strategy for Covalence claims keeps existing article functionality during claims layer activation to avoid a big-bang cutover.
Covalence v2 is designed as a 'computational epistemology engine' that answers four questions simultaneously: belief quantification, gap awareness, active acquisition, and principled forgetting.

The Covalence v2 architecture is grounded in five theoretical frameworks: Free Energy Principle (objective function), AGM Belief Revision (update logic), Stigmergy (coordination model), Pearl's Causal Hierarchy (depth layer), and Complementary Learning Systems (memory architecture).

An adversarial review of the Covalence v2 architecture specification identified six mathematical and engineering weaknesses in the original document, which were subsequently addressed in a revised version.

Damped cascade retraction in Covalence v2 uses geometric decay (α=0.65), epsilon threshold (ε=0.01), budget cap (500 nodes), and max-hop limit (H=8 hops), providing analytical guarantee of approximately 25 nodes visited on average.

Temporal Knowledge Graphs (TKGs) extend standard KG triples (head, relation, tail) to quadruples (head, relation, tail, timestamp), where timestamp can be an interval [t_start, t_end] or a point timestamp.

TKG reasoning includes three task types: interpolation (completing unknown facts within the known time range), extrapolation (forecasting facts at time T+1), and temporal QA (answering time-scoped questions).
RDF-star (W3C working draft) annotates facts with temporal metadata more compactly than RDF reification, requiring fewer triples per annotated fact.
Kim et al. (2024/2026) found that RDF-star quadruples yield more informative memory despite greater storage cost per fact, because temporal context enables better reasoning.
CFKGR (Counterfactual Knowledge Graph Reasoning, Zellinger et al., EACL 2024) frames counterfactual reasoning as adding hypothetical edges to a KG and inferring downstream graph structure changes.

COULDD (COUnterfactual Learning from Data Dependencies) fine-tunes KG embeddings on hypothetical facts, enabling the model to propagate counterfactual consequences through learned structural patterns.
ChatGPT outperforms KGEs at detecting plausible counterfactual changes in KGs, but suffers from poor knowledge retention by hallucinating away original (non-hypothetical) facts.

AnyBURL (fully symbolic, rule-based) achieves performance matching RotatE on WN18RR benchmark (~0.478 MRR vs 0.476), suggesting symbolic methods are competitive with or superior to embeddings at structured-graph scale.

AnyBURL achieves MRR of 0.478 on WN18RR, matching ComplEx (0.480) and exceeding RotatE (0.476), demonstrating that symbolic rule-based methods are competitive with embedding approaches on structured knowledge graphs.

source_title: covalence#173 — Surgical Pre-Claims Refactoring Spec

claims.text: Covalence issue covalence#173 is ready for review as of 2026-03-04.
The proposed change in Covalence includes promoting parse_json_response to a shared worker module helper.
The proposed change in Covalence includes moving select_by_trust_recency to a shared worker location.
The proposed change in Covalence includes extracting the post-compile follow-up task sequence into a named function.
The proposed change in Covalence includes adding a node_type parameter to enqueue_due_consolidations.
The proposed change in Covalence includes adding 7 new EdgeType variants for claims.
The proposed change in Covalence includes generalizing symmetric-edge enforcement in EdgeService.
The proposed change in Covalence includes replacing hardcoded SQL edge-type lists with type-level helpers.
The proposed change in Covalence includes renaming ConfidenceResult.article_id to node_id.
The proposed change in Covalence includes extracting fetch provenance inputs from compute confidence.

source_title: PKM/Note-Taking Systems: Link Suggestion, Backlink Inference, and Graph Maintenance Patterns

claims.text: Obsidian uses exact string matching against note titles and aliases for its 'Unlinked Mentions' feature.
Roam Research automatically creates bidirectional links for every page reference or tag.
Logseq's Automatic Linker plugin monitors typed text and auto-converts matching terms to page references.
Notion does not have automatic link suggestion or unlinked mentions features natively.
Obsidian's Graph Analysis Plugin implements co-citation analysis to identify topical relationships between notes.
The Jaccard Similarity formula measures how similar two notes' link neighborhoods are.
Obsidian Smart Connections Plugin uses an embedding model to compute cosine similarity between notes.
Roam Research allows block references using the '(())' syntax for granular connections.
Logseq uses the same '(())' syntax as Roam for block-level references.
Obsidian's block references require a 'block-id' identifier.
The Obsidian Community has requested a feature for typed links or semantic links since 2020.
Roam Research's attribute syntax can encode typed relationships on blocks.
The Adamic-Adar link prediction method weights shared neighbors by the inverse log of their degree.
Obsidian's Smart Connections Plugin supports over 100 embedding models via API.

source_title: Claims Architecture Spec v2 — Complete Revision with All Reviews Incorporated

claims.text: The Claims Architecture Spec v2 was fully revised on March 4, 2026.
Claims are atomic assertions compiled from articles in the semantic domain.
The nodes table uses 'node_type = claim' to store claims.
Claims have a status that can be 'active', 'settled', 'archived', or 'superseded'.
Claims are extracted from node sections, not raw sources.
The confidence of a claim is calculated using the formula conf = 1 - ∏(1 - rᵢ) for the N exemplar sources.
Claims can be archived if they have zero EXTRACTED_FROM edges and zero CONTAINS edges.
The extraction process for claims includes a cosine similarity check against existing claims.
Claims can be settled when their confidence exceeds 0.999.

source_title: Cache Invalidation Strategies Across Distributed Systems — Patterns for Knowledge Graph Caching

claims.text: Cache invalidation is one of the two hard things in computer science.
TTL-based invalidation guarantees an upper bound on staleness.
Choosing TTL for cache invalidation is heuristic.
Event-driven invalidation requires careful tracking of all cache keys affected by each data change.
Version-based invalidation allows for O(1) invalidation for entire namespaces.
Tag-based invalidation allows for surgical precision in cache invalidation.
The dependency graph approach requires explicit dependency registration.
The cache stampede problem occurs when a popular cached item expires.
Probabilistic early expiration is a mathematically optimal solution to the cache stampede problem.
As of October 2025, Cloudflare achieves global purge-by-tag latency under 150ms.

source_title: covalence#161 — Provenance Cap & Auto-Split Implementation Spec

claims.text: Covalence issue covalence#161 is ready for implementation.
The recommended cap for provenance fan-in during article_compile is PROVENANCE_CAP = 40.
The auto_split threshold is set at PROVENANCE_SPLIT_THRESHOLD = 50.
The top-20 articles each have 524–676 ORIGINATES edges.
If the count of ORIGINATES edges exceeds PROVENANCE_SPLIT_THRESHOLD, an auto_split task is enqueued.
The auto_split task type is added to the slow_path_queue.
The algorithm for determining split boundary uses mini-batch k-means with k=2.
The embedding availability guard excludes sources without embeddings from centroid computation.
The primary assignment of sources to child articles is done via k-means clustering.
The original article is archived after the auto_split process.

source_title: Incremental Compilation in Build Systems: Dependency Tracking and Minimal Rebuild Patterns (Bazel, Buck2, Gradle, rustc/Salsa)

claims.text: Bazel's evaluation framework is called Skyframe.
Bazel uses Merkle trees with SHA-256 digests for its remote cache.
DICE is the Deterministic Incremental Computation Engine used in Buck2.
DICE tracks reverse dependencies explicitly.
The Rust compiler structures all compilation as a query system.
The Red-Green Algorithm is used in the Rust compiler for incremental compilation.
Gradle checks if task inputs have changed using content hashes before executing a task.
Gradle supports a remote build cache that is content-addressable.
All four systems use content hashing to detect input changes.
Bazel does not use a top-down invalidation strategy.

source_title: Materialized View Maintenance in Databases: Comprehensive Research (Oracle, PostgreSQL, Databricks, Academic IVM)

claims.text: A materialized view (MV) is a cached/precomputed result of a query.
Oracle supports refreshing materialized views with the command CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH ON COMMIT.
PostgreSQL's pg_ivm extension provides incremental refresh via AFTER triggers and transition tables.
Databricks allows manual refresh or scheduled refresh of materialized views via pipelines.
Oracle's FULL REFRESH requires dropping or truncating the materialized view and re-executing the defining query from scratch.
PostgreSQL's only built-in option for refreshing materialized views is FULL REFRESH.
The Counting Algorithm maintains views incrementally by storing an additional COUNT column for each tuple.
DBToaster introduced higher-order delta processing for incremental view maintenance.
DBSP provides a clean algebraic framework for incremental view maintenance.
Incrementability is defined as benefit(IVM) / cost(IVM) according to Tang et al.

source_title: Claims Layer Spec — Blue-Green Migration (covalence#036)

claims.text: Claims are nodes in the existing nodes table.
The existing schema uses a unified nodes table with node_type IN ('article', 'source', 'entity').
Claims fit naturally as node_type = 'claim'.
The edge types already support it: We add CONTAINS and SUPPORTS.
Claims use existing columns in the nodes table.
The generation column is added to track blue-green migration.
The confidence pipeline becomes claim-level after migration.
Claims are extracted from sources and inserted as nodes with node_type='claim'.
The migration process includes a validation gate to ensure coverage.
The rollback plan is tested and fully reversible.

source_title: Spec: Article-to-Article Semantic Edge Inference — covalence#160

claims.text: Covalence articles currently only carry ORIGINATES/provenance edges linking them to their source documents.
The semantic graph in Covalence lives entirely in source space.
The inference pipeline in Covalence is designed to populate direct article-to-article semantic edges.
The first tier of edge inference in Covalence uses Jaccard similarity of article_sources sets.
A Jaccard threshold of 0.3 is required to insert a RELATES_TO edge in Covalence.
The second tier of edge inference in Covalence uses array overlap on the domain_path column.
A threshold of 2 shared tags is required to insert a RELATES_TO edge in Covalence's second tier.
The third tier of edge inference in Covalence uses ANN search via pgvector against articles.embedding.
A cosine similarity threshold of 0.65 is required to insert a RELATES_TO edge in Covalence's third tier.
The fourth tier of edge inference in Covalence uses LLM judgment on article pairs.
The new task type infer_article_edges is added to the slow_path_queue in Covalence.
The composite index on knowledge_edges(edge_type, source_node_id, target_node_id) must exist in Covalence.
The migration file migrations/037_infer_article_edges_task_type.sql is created to add the new task type in Covalence.
The backfill pass in Covalence enqueues one task per article that has no outbound semantic edges to other articles.
The handle_infer_article_edges function is located in engine/src/worker/infer_article_edges.rs in Covalence.
The configuration struct InferenceConfig in Covalence includes a tier1_jaccard_threshold with a default of 0.3.
Covalence logs each inferred edge at DEBUG level.
Covalence emits a metric counter for inferred edges with tier and edge_type.
The acceptance criteria for Covalence include that an infer_article_edges task appears in slow_path_queue after article_compile.

source_title: Claims Architecture Design Session — Complete Capture (2026-03-04)

claims.text: The Claims Architecture Design Session took place on March 4, 2026.
Ten parallel research workers were spawned across various domains including build systems and cache invalidation.
The Output Equality Firewall is the single biggest optimization in the claims architecture.
Claims are atomic, structured, and hashable assertions.
The blue-green migration strategy includes an atomic cutover to activate generation 2.
Claims should attribute to specific node_sections for verifiability.
The current system state includes 1,606 active nodes and 487 articles.
The confidence propagation at the article level is too coarse.
Claims enable surgical updates by allowing source changes to affect specific claims.

source_title: PKM Systems: Link Suggestion & Graph Maintenance Research Summary

claims.text: Obsidian uses explicit user-created links in the format `[[Note Title]]` or `[[Note Title|alias]]`.
Roam Research uses explicit user-created links in the format `[[Page Name]]` and also supports implicit page links with `#tag`.
Logseq uses explicit user-created links in the format `[[Page Link]]` and block-level references with `((block-uuid))`.
All three systems, Obsidian, Roam Research, and Logseq, create bidirectional edges automatically linking Note A to Note B.
Obsidian shows unlinked mentions in its Backlinks panel, allowing one-click conversion to a real link.
Roam Research displays unlinked references at the bottom of each page, which users can click to promote to linked.
Logseq has a feature for unlinked references, with a community plugin that automates bulk promotion.
Obsidian's 'Smart Connections' plugin uses a local embedding model to compute embeddings for each note on save.
The 'Smart Connections' plugin in Obsidian shows the top-N most similar notes by cosine similarity of embeddings.
Roam Research's graph view can show unlinked references as dashed edges upon feature requests.

source_title: Synaptic Tagging and Capture (STC): Mechanism for Selective Memory Consolidation — Comprehensive Review (Frey & Morris 1997 through 2025)

claims.text: The Synaptic Tagging and Capture (STC) mechanism is involved in selective memory consolidation.
Long-term potentiation (LTP) has two phases: early-LTP (e-LTP) and late-LTP (l-LTP).
Early-LTP (e-LTP) lasts approximately 1-3 hours and is protein synthesis-independent.
Late-LTP (l-LTP) lasts days to weeks and is protein synthesis-dependent.
Synaptic tagging sets a local tag that lasts approximately 30-90 minutes ex vivo.
Protein synthesis is the gating factor for converting e-LTP into l-LTP.
Behavioral tagging (BT) allows weak learning experiences to be consolidated into long-term memory if followed by a strong event.
The tagging window for STC is approximately 30-90 minutes ex vivo and can be extended up to 9 hours.
Clustering of synaptic inputs enhances the efficiency of triggering and capturing PRP synthesis.
Memories require a second wave of protein synthesis approximately 12-24 hours after initial consolidation for full persistence.

source_title: Incremental Build Systems: Dependency Tracking and Minimal Rebuild Patterns (Research Summary)

claims.text: Incremental build systems aim to rebuild only the minimal set of outputs when inputs change.
Incremental build systems operate on a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of dependencies.
Bazel requires developers to declare all direct dependencies in BUILD files.
The DAG enables parallelism and incremental rebuilding.
Buck and Bazel use Merkle-tree build graphs where each node's hash is derived from its inputs' hashes.
Forward propagation dirty-marking algorithms mark all downstream nodes dirty when an input changes.
Lazy demand-driven algorithms check if any dependencies have changed when a consumer queries a derived value.
Version-based invalidation in Salsa increments a global revision counter with each input change.
Content hashing in Bazel uses SHA-256 or similar to hash each input.
Gradle normalizes inputs before hashing to ensure semantically equivalent inputs produce the same cache key.
Salsa is a framework for on-demand incrementalized computation.
Gradle performs up-to-date checks by fingerprinting all declared inputs and outputs.
Buck distinguishes between rule keys to decide cache hits.

source_title: Reactive Patterns Applied to Knowledge Graph Change Propagation

claims.text: A knowledge graph is structurally analogous to a reactive computation graph.
In a knowledge graph, articles/facts are represented as nodes.
In a knowledge graph, provenance/dependency links are represented as edges.
When a source changes, the knowledge system needs to determine which articles are affected and recompute them.
The dependency graph in a knowledge graph can be maintained to determine which articles need recompilation when a source is updated.
Covalence's usage_score captures the frequency of article queries.
Dynamic dependency discovery allows for the re-establishment of dependencies at compilation time.
Fine-grained reactivity allows for tracking changes at a finer granularity than whole sources.
Stabilization epochs can be used to ensure consistency during batch updates in a knowledge graph.
Delta propagation allows for targeted updates instead of full recompilation of articles.

source_title: Cross-Domain Research Synthesis: 8 Domains Applied to Covalence Knowledge Graph

claims.text: The Dependency DAG is the core abstraction in Bazel, where sources are inputs, articles are derived artifacts, and provenance links are edges.
Bazel uses content hashing instead of timestamps to skip recompilation if the source content_hash is unchanged.
Salsa employs demand-driven invalidation, recompiling dirty articles only on query and tracking verified_at_revision per article.
Bazel's output equality firewall optimization stops cascading recompilation if the article content is approximately equal to the old version after recompilation.
Bazel's hermetic compilation ensures that output depends only on declared inputs and the compilation prompt.
PKM systems like Obsidian, Roam, and Logseq utilize tiered link inference, which includes lexical, structural, and semantic levels.
PKM systems maintain a cosine similarity floor of approximately 0.65 for semantic links.
Event sourcing systems treat sources as immutable, append-only events and articles as projections or materialized views.
Materialized views in databases use a counting algorithm to track how many sources support each claim.
Cache invalidation can utilize tag-based invalidation via provenance, where the source UUID serves as the tag.
Reactive dataflow systems like Excel and MobX implement a mark-then-sweep strategy to manage stale flags and recomputation.
Neural plasticity principles suggest that the hippocampus processes fast, episodic information while the neocortex handles slow, consolidated knowledge.
Merge and conflict resolution in systems like Git utilize a three-way merge approach to detect conflicts.

source_title: Fine-Grained Reactivity: MobX, Signals, SolidJS, and Svelte Internals

claims.text: MobX makes observable state values via Proxy/Object.defineProperty.
Computed values in MobX are derived from observables via pure functions and are cached until inputs change.
Reactions in MobX are side effects that run when observed values change.
Actions in MobX are the only way to modify state and batch mutations into atomic transactions.
MobX tracks dependencies at runtime by intercepting property access.
MobX achieves glitch-free updates through a two-phase transaction model.
MobX automatically prunes the dependency graph when a computed is no longer observed by any reaction.
SolidJS uses signals as reactive values with getter/setter.
In SolidJS, effects/reactions are side-effecting computations that re-run when dependencies change.
Svelte 5 moved to a signals-based reactivity model called 'runes'.

source_title: Complete Cross-Domain Research: 10 Domains → 7 Design Principles for Covalence

claims.text: Covalence uses an immutable segment architecture to write new sources quickly and defer restructuring to background maintenance.
Covalence employs a TieredMergePolicy to group articles by size tier and merge when the count in a tier exceeds a threshold.
Covalence uses single-pass clustering for source to article assignment based on similarity thresholds.
Covalence maintains compact article summary vectors for merge and split decisions using BIRCH CF-tree insights.
Covalence creates new articles under write pressure and merges under query pressure when the fan-out cost exceeds the merge cost.
Covalence implements a push-pull hybrid approach for supply chain disruption propagation.
Covalence uses geometric decay damping to stop propagating disruption signals when they fall below a minimum threshold.
Covalence employs dual sourcing to maintain confidence in articles despite single source deletions.
Covalence serves stale articles while recompilation runs using buffer inventory.
Covalence tracks how many independent sources support each article claim using a reference-counted provenance system.
Covalence requires a higher evidence threshold to override well-established articles to protect high-confidence articles.
Covalence processes the mutation queue in ascending height order to prevent glitches during propagation.
Covalence splits articles when there are more than 15 sources or high contention density.
Covalence merges articles when there is more than 60% semantic overlap or when near-eviction and small size conditions are met.

source_title: Reactive Programming & Dataflow Systems — Synthesis Summary (2026-03-04)

claims.text: All reactive/dataflow systems share one abstraction: a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes are computations and edges are dependency relationships.
Excel's Dependency Engine is the canonical spreadsheet recalculation model.
MobX implements a fine-grained reactive object graph with three primitives: observables, computed values, and reactions.
Jane Street's Incremental library is a typed, DAG-based incremental computation framework.
Glitches arise in the diamond pattern when a node is pushed eagerly after one input updates but before another input updates.
Most production systems use a push-pull hybrid: push cheap invalidation markers, pull actual recomputation only when needed.
A knowledge graph maps cleanly onto the reactive model.
The mutation_queue in Covalence implements the staging layer for marking dependent articles as STALE.
The cutoff optimization in knowledge graph recompilation is critical because most source edits are local.

source_title: Jane Street Incremental: Self-Adjusting Computation for Efficient Change Propagation

claims.text: Incremental is a library for building self-adjusting computations (SAC).
Self-adjusting computations update efficiently when inputs change.
Incremental supports bind, allowing the computation structure of the graph to change.
The stabilize() function must be called to trigger recomputation in Incremental.
Each node in Incremental can have a custom cutoff function to stop change propagation.
Incremental uses a height-based priority queue for stabilization.
Incremental only computes nodes that are transitively required by some observer.
Incremental is related to Adapton, another SAC library from academia.
Incremental is used for risk calculations that depend on live market data and user configurations.
Incremental is used for GUI construction to ensure only changed parts re-render.
